title: G.Veeranagouda S/O Late Linganna vs Smt.Veeramma W/O Peerappa on 28 August, 2017
body: G.Veeranagouda S/O Late Linganna vs Smt.Veeramma W/O Peerappa on 28 August, 2017 Author: K.N.Phaneendra Bench: K.N.Phaneendra 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA KALABURAGI BENCH DATED THIS THE 28TH DAY OF AUGUST 2017 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.N.PHANEENDRA REGULAR SECOND APPEAL No.7235/2010 Between: 1. G. Veeranagouda S/o Late Linganna Aged about 55 years Occ: Agriculture R/o Halli Hosur (Sanna Hosur) Tq. Manvi-584 123 2. Sharanabasava S/o Basappa Aged about 25 years Occ: Agriculture R/o Halli Hosur (Sanna Hosur) Tq. Manvi-584 123 ... Appellants (By Sri Ashok S. Kinagi, Advocate) And: Smt. Veeramma W/o Peerappa Aged about 46 years Occ: Householder R/o Kalmala, Tq. Manvi 2 Now residing at Netajinagar Raichur-584 102 ... Respondent (By Sri Ameet Kumar Deshpande, Advocate) This Regular Second Appeal is filed under Section 100 of Code of Civil Procedure praying to allow the appeal and set aside the judgment and decree dated 06.01.2010 passed by the Court of Additional District Judge, Raichur, in R.A.No.20/2008 and also the judgment and decree dated 04.01.2008 passed by the Court of the Principal Civil Judge (Senior Division) at Raichur, in O.S.No.150/2004 and dismiss the suit of the plaintiff in the Trial Court with costs throughout. This appeal coming on for Orders this day, the Court delivered the following: JUDGMENT Heard the learned counsel for the appellants and the learned counsel appearing for the respondent. Perused the judgment and decree passed by the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court. 2. For the purpose of easy understanding and convenience, ranks of the parties as per their ranks before the Trial Court is retained. 3 3. Appellant No.1 herein, Sri Veeranagouda is the first defendant before the Trial Court. The respondent herein, Smt. Veeramma is the plaintiff. There is no dispute that the plaintiff and the first defendant are the daughter and son of late G.Linganna who died intestate. It is the claim of the plaintiff that the suit schedule properties are the properties of her father who died intestate and some of the properties particularly, Sy.Nos.94/A and 105/A are the properties originally belonged to the grand mother of the plaintiff and the first defendant by name Neelamma who died prior to the death of Linganna. The other suit properties i.e., Sy.Nos.38/A/4, 47/AA/2 and 47/A/3 are the properties purchased by Linganna during his lifetime and thereby, all the properties are the properties of late Linganna and as such, after the death of Linganna who died intestate, the plaintiff is entitled for half share in the suit schedule properties. It is also contended that out of 7 acres 19 guntas in Sy.No.105/A, 5 acres of land have been sold 4 by the first defendant in favour of the second defendant- Sharanabasava S/o Basappa and the sale deed is not binding upon the plaintiff. 4. Though the first defendant who appeared before the Court has admitted the relationship, he has taken up the contention that the plaintiff is not entitled for any share in the suit schedule properties. He contends that, according to law, she is not entitled for any share in the suit schedule properties by virtue of the Karnataka Amendment Act No.23 of 1994 Section 6A clause (d) and the plaintiff cannot claim any right or interest over the properties. There is no much dispute with regard to the nature of the properties and also acquisition of properties by the father of plaintiff and the first defendant. The first defendant also admits that his father had purchased the said properties. Further, it is contended that, suit item Nos.1 and 2 are the properties belonged to the said Smt. Neelamma. She 5 gave that property to the first Defendant by virtue of mutation. But he never stated as to how he acquired those properties, according to law, either by way of gift, Will or any other testamentary documents by the said lady. On the basis of the above rival contentions of the parties, the Trial Court has framed the following issues: 1. Whether the plaintiff proves that the suit schedule properties are the joint family ancestral properties of herself and the defendants and that she is in joint possession of the same? 2. Whether she further proves that the sale deed executed by defendant no.1 in favour of defendant no.2 is without any consideration and it is done only to deprive her share and as such it is not binding on her? 3. Whether the plaintiff is entitled to any share in the suit properties? If so to what share is she entitled? 4. Whether the plaintiff is entitled to the relief of declaration as prayed? 5. What decree or order? 6 5. The plaintiff examined herself as PW.1 and got marked the documents at Exs.P-1 to P-9. The first defendant examined himself as DW.1 and got marked the documents at Exs.D-1 to D-15. The Trial Court after appreciating the oral and documentary evidence on record, answered Issue Nos.1 to 4 in the 'Affirmative' and decreed the suit of the plaintiff, granting half share in the suit schedule properties equal to that of the first defendant. 6. Being aggrieved by the said judgment and decree, the first and second defendants have preferred an appeal before the Additional District Judge at Raichur, in R.A.No.20/2008. The First Appellate Court considering the grounds of the appeal and also the findings given by the Trial Court on the issues framed by it and also considering the evidence on record has framed the following points for consideration; 7 1. Whether the plaintiff is entitled for a share in the suit properties? 2. Whether she further proves that the sale made by Deft.No.1 in favour of Deft.No.2 of one of the items of suit properties is not binding on her? 3. Whether the finding recorded by the trial court requires interference? 4. What order? 7. The First Appellate Court after re-assessing the oral and documentary evidence has come to the same conclusion as that of the Trial Court and confirmed the judgment and decree passed by the Trial Court and consequently, dismissed the appeal. 8. On careful perusal of the judgments of the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court, it would indicate that virtually no legal issue has been raised in any manner. Though the first defendant has stated that as 8 per Section 6A Clause (d) of the Karnataka Amendment to the Succession Act, the plaintiff is not entitled for any share, as the pleadings of the parties go to show that the properties are virtually self-acquired properties of the father of plaintiff and the first defendant, there is no question of Section 6A coming in the way of plaintiff claiming her share in the properties. It is not in dispute that the said Linganna, father of the plaintiff and the first defendant died in the year 2001 and the suit is filed in the year 2004. It is not disputed by the first defendant that his father died intestate. The First Appellate Court and the Trial Court have consistently considered the properties involved in this particular case. The first defendant has claimed the properties of his grand mother Smt. Neelamma on the basis of mutation. But the First Appellate Court has categorically discussed this particular aspect and came to the conclusion that there is no testamentary instruments by Smt. Neelamma or any documents 9 which could convey right, title and interest over the properties in favour of the first defendant. Therefore, though the first defendant has taken up the contention that the first defendant himself has acquired the properties and possession of the properties of his grand mother, it can be construed that the said properties are also the properties belonging to Late Linganna who virtually succeeded to the properties of his mother and thereby, he became the absolute owner of the said properties. I do not find any mistake being committed by both the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court in treating the said properties also as the properties belonged to the deceased-Linganna. 9. The learned counsel for the appellant has tried to convince this Court that after the death of Smt. Neelamma, the properties went to the hands of Linganna who is the father of the first defendant and out of the income of the said properties, the said 10 Linganna purchased the subsequent properties. Therefore, the said properties are all joint family properties and thereby, the plaintiff is not entitled for half share in those properties and further, he submitted that the plaintiff has to establish before the Court that all the properties are the exclusive properties of Linganna who died intestate. Therefore, the plaintiff herself has not established before the Court that all the properties are the exclusive properties of Late Linganna and the circumstances go to show that they are the joint family properties. In my opinion, this argument of the learned counsel is not supported either by the pleadings of the defendants or by the pleadings of the plaintiff and no evidence has been adduced in this context. As could be seen from the way in which the parties have proceeded before the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court, they have treated all the properties belonged to Late Linganna and not the first defendant exclusively that is to say the first defendant claims the properties of 11 his father because he has looked after his father during his last days and spent lot of money towards his medical expenses. Consequently, he claims the properties of his grand mother on the basis of acceptance of mutation and saying that his grand mother has given those properties to him directly. Apart from the above pleadings and evidence, the defendants never stated that the said properties are the joint family properties so that the plaintiff is not entitled for such a share as awarded by the Trial Court or the First Appellate Court. 10. Therefore, on looking to the pleadings and evidence adduced by the parties, the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court have clearly appreciated the legal aspects and also the factual aspects that the said properties are the properties which were in the hands of Late Linganna and he was the owner of those properties who acquired the said properties from his mother and 12 as well as by his private purchase. Therefore, the plaintiff is also entitled for her equal share with that of the first defendant. In my opinion, no illegality has been committed either by the Trial Court or by the First Appellate Court. 11. Under the above circumstances, no substantial question of law arises for consideration of this Court. 12. Hence, I pass the following: ORDER The appeal is dismissed as no substantial question of law arises for consideration. Considering the relationship between the parties, no order as to costs. Sd/- JUDGE NB*/SNC
